plainMeaning: I bow to all the Tirthankaras who are yet to be born in the future, whatever their names will turn out to be.
recitation: anāgata jinavara, hośo tehnā nāma. bhaṇo 'vācaka mūlā', tehne karuṁ praṇāma.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit an- (not) + āgata (come, past participle of ā-√gam, 'to arrive'); literally 'not-yet-arrived', hence 'future'.
wordByWord.notes: Here it modifies the Jinas of the coming ascending half-cycle, the anāgata caubīsī that dhāla 3 has been naming.

wordByWord.gloss: the excellent Jinas, foremost of conquerors (Tirthankaras)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jina ('conqueror') + vara ('best, excellent'); a honorific intensifier giving 'the best of Jinas'.
wordByWord.notes: Singular form used collectively for the whole future set of twenty-four.

wordByWord.gloss: (who) shall be, will come to be
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati future of હોવું (hovuṁ, 'to be'), root હો- (ho-, from Indo-Aryan *bhū-) + future suffix -śo.
wordByWord.notes: Future tense is deliberate: these Jinas have not yet arisen, so the worshipper honours beings who are still to come.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati genitive of demonstrative તેહ (teha, 'that/those'), from Sanskrit tasya / teṣām ('of him / of them').
Sanskrit nāman ('name'); the stem nāma carried into Prakrit and Gujarati unchanged.
wordByWord.notes: Left unspecified on purpose — the verse closes the future-caubīsī section without listing each name.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit-Prakrit root √bhaṇ ('to speak, recite'), cf. Prakrit bhaṇai, Gujarati ભણવું (bhaṇvuṁ); imperative/3sg form ભણો.
wordByWord.notes: Frames the poet's signature: either an imperative to the reciter ('recite!') or the formula 'thus says Vācaka Mūlā'.

wordByWord.etymology: vācaka, the Upādhyāya monastic rank ('reader/preceptor'), + the personal name Mūlā; the bhaṇitā (signature) sealing the dhāla.
wordByWord.notes: Quoted verbatim in the source with quotation marks; it is the recurring chhāp that closes each melodic section.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati dative of તેહ (teha, 'those'), from Sanskrit oblique tad-/teṣām; dative-accusative marking the object of the bow.
Sanskrit-Prakrit root √kṛ ('to do, make'), 1sg present karomi > Gujarati કરું (karuṁ, 'I do').
wordByWord.notes: First-person voice personalises the act of veneration.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pra- (forth, down) + √nam ('to bend, bow'); 'a bending-down', i.e. salutation/prostration.
wordByWord.notes: karuṁ praṇāma = 'I make obeisance', the act that consummates the whole future-caubīsī invocation.

literalTranslation: The future excellent Jinas — whatever their names shall be — Vācaka Mūlā says (recite): to them I make obeisance.
idiomaticTranslation: To the foremost Jinas yet to come, whatever names they shall bear, Vācaka Mūlā now bows: to them I offer my reverent salutation.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This couplet seals the future-caubīsī movement. Having just bowed to the named future Jinas (Samādhi, Susaṁvara, Yaśodhara, Vijaya, Mallideva, Anantavīrya, Bhadrakṛt in the preceding verse), the poet now sweeps up every remaining future Tirthankara in one gesture: 'whatever their names shall be (hośo tehnā nāma), to them I bow.' The deliberate vagueness of 'nāma' is the point — the worshipper does not need each name to revere the office; he salutes the very fact that Jinas will continue to arise. The future tense hośo carries the verse's whole theology: praṇāma is offered to beings who do not yet exist. The closing line is the bhaṇitā — the recurring 'Vācaka Mūlā says' that stitches each dhāla — here fused with the act of bowing (tehne karuṁ praṇāma), so that the poet's signature and the worshipper's salutation become a single utterance. With this praṇāma the third caubīsī (anāgata) is complete, and the hymn is poised to turn from the Jinas of the three ages toward the living Jinas of Mahāvideha.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse expresses a striking facet of Jain samyaktva: reverence is owed to Jina-hood as a timeless principle, not merely to historically risen teachers. Bowing to Jinas who are still anāgata affirms that the dharma is anādi-ananta (beginningless and endless) and that the Jina-lineage never fails across the turning half-cycles (utsarpiṇī/avasarpiṇī). Honouring the unborn future Tirthankaras — whose names tradition does record (Padmanābha first, Bhadrakṛt last) yet which the verse leaves open — cultivates faith independent of particulars, a pure veneration of the awakened state itself.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter uses this verse to stretch devotion beyond the familiar twenty-four of the present age: at dawn one bows even to teachers not yet born, training the mind in a reverence that asks nothing in return and clings to no name. It is a quiet exercise in faith — honouring what one will never personally meet — and a fitting seal before the hymn moves on to the ever-present Mahāvideha Jinas.

crossReferences.text: Anāgata caubīsī — the twenty-four future Tirthankaras (Padmanābha … Bhadrakṛt)
crossReferences.source: Jain cosmographic tradition; Padmanābha's Anāgata-bhāvi Tīrthaṅkara list

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Cauvīsattho) — model of bowing to the Tirthankaras by enumeration
crossReferences.source: Āvaśyaka-sūtra, as in this book's Devasi sequence

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Root glosses and etymologies for anāgata, jina/jinavara, nāman, √bhaṇ, √kṛ and praṇāma (pra-√nam).

sources.title: Jain Tirthankara — Past, Present, Future (Jainsite)
sources.consultedFor: The list of the twenty-four anāgata (future) Tirthankaras to identify the set this verse gathers up.

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: The doctrine of successive caubīsīs across half-cycles and the concept of future Tirthankaras.

notes: The signature line 'ભણો વાચક મૂલા' is read two compatible ways: as an imperative to the reciter ('recite!') or as the bhaṇitā formula 'thus says Vācaka Mūlā'; both are rendered. 'હોશો' is the Old Gujarati future of હોવું ('to be'), here meaning 'shall be'. 'નામ' is deliberately left unspecified by the verse, so no individual future-Jina names are asserted from it; the names cited in the doctrinal note come from the wider tradition, not from this couplet. Demonstratives 'તેહના/તેહને' are Old Gujarati oblique/dative forms of તેહ. Spelling and quotation marks preserved verbatim from the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition.
